does: This paper presents a systematic red-teaming evaluation of six production coding agents (Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot, Windsurf, Cline, Trae) that execute tasks by invoking tools. The study unfolds in two phases: Phase 1 discovers ToolLeak, a vulnerability enabling malicious extraction of system prompts through tool argument retrieval, and demonstrates successful prompt recovery across all tested agents. Phase 2 demonstrates remote code execution attacks via two-channel prompt injection—simultaneously poisoning tool descriptions and return values—with payloads adaptively crafted using information leaked in Phase 1. The authors conduct emulation-based testing across multiple configurations to validate the attacks' effectiveness and scope.
connects: This work extends a growing body of research on adversarial attacks against language model agents. Prior studies have demonstrated prompt injection and jailbreaking attacks on general-purpose and domain-specific agents, but coding agents present a distinct attack surface through tool invocation mechanisms. The paper builds on established red-teaming methodologies while addressing a gap: the specific vulnerabilities introduced when agents invoke external tools to write, modify, and execute code. It connects to research on prompt leakage, injection attacks, and LLM security, while introducing the novel perspective that tool-mediated agent interaction creates new exploitation paths distinct from direct prompt manipulation.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for securing tool-invocational coding agents and opens multiple research directions. It motivates the development of robustness mechanisms—such as tool sandboxing, input validation, and prompt hardening—to mitigate tool-based injection attacks. The discovered vulnerabilities highlight the need for principled design patterns in agent-tool interfaces that prevent information leakage and prompt poisoning. The systematic evaluation framework enables future work to assess new defensive strategies and to evaluate emerging coding agents as they appear. The findings suggest research into formal verification of tool invocation behavior, adversarial training methods for agent resilience, and architectural changes to prevent tool descriptions and returns from serving as injection vectors.
